label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, so the user is asking if a man can leave all his money to his widow's sister, who he plans to marry next year. Let me break this down step by step. First, the man is currently married, and he's leaving his money to his widow's sister. Wait, but he's alive and plans to marry someone else next year. So, the person he's leaving the money to is the sister of his current wife. That sister is not his step-sister yet, but his future wife's sister. But he's not married to that sister yet—he's going to marry the current wife's sister in the future. But here's the catch: when making a will, the person has to be alive and have the capacity to make the will. The man is alive now, and he's making a provision in his will to leave money to someone who he intends to marry. But the key here is whether the legal system recognizes such a provision when the marriage hasn't taken place yet. In many jurisdictions, a will can include conditional bequests. For example, if person A is alive, leave the money to them; if not, then to person B. But in this case, the condition is that he marries the sister. However, marriage is a future event, and the will is a legal document that takes effect after death. So, if the man dies before marrying the sister, the bequest to her would be invalid because the condition hasn't been met. If he marries her after making the will but before his death, then the bequest might still be valid, depending on the jurisdiction. Another angle: if he leaves his entire estate to his future wife's sister, and if that sister is not his blood relation (assuming the current wife is not his blood relative), then there might be issues with the will's validity if the sister predeceases him or if the marriage doesn't happen. Also, some jurisdictions require that the beneficiaries named in a will be alive at the time the will is executed, or the bequest lapses.
